planting.note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring; sweet peas thrive in cool weather.

directions: Nick or soak the hard seeds overnight to speed germination, then sow directly 1 inch deep in cool, moist, well-drained soil in early spring. This tall vining variety produces intensely fragrant cream flowers edged in lavender, so provide a trellis or netting 5 to 6 feet tall for support. Keep roots cool and evenly moist, and cut blooms frequently to prolong flowering. Plants fade in summer heat, so start them early where summers are hot.

planting.note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seeds shallowly, barely covered, in warm soil after the last frost, or start indoors a few weeks early. Upright plumes of caramel-brown seed heads rise on sturdy stems and make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers, especially for autumn arrangements. Heat-tolerant and low-maintenance, this amaranth thrives in full sun and average soil. Cut plumes for drying just as they fully color, and expect self-sowing if seed heads are left in place.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; needs a long, warm season.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, transplanting once the weather is warm. This dwarf variety features dramatic bronze-red foliage and scarlet seed heads that give a bold tropical effect. It is heat- and drought-tolerant once established and attracts hummingbirds. CAUTION: all plant parts, especially the seeds, are highly poisonous if ingested, so keep away from children and pets.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost date; prefers cool conditions for best blooms.

directions: Direct sow shallowly in well-drained soil around the last frost date, as sweet sultan resents transplanting. Airy, softly domed 2 to 3 inch fragrant blooms sway on long, graceful stems and draw bees to the garden. Give plants full sun and average, well-drained soil, and stake in windy sites. Cut flowers just as they open for long-lasting, romantic bouquets, and deadhead to extend the display.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost; can also be fall sown in mild climates.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds, pressing them into the soil since light aids germination, after the last frost. Tall, bushy plants produce an abundance of swirled bicolor blooms that draw butterflies and bees to a pollinator or cutting garden. Coreopsis is quick, easy, and drought-tolerant, thriving in full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Deadhead regularly to keep blooms coming through the season, and mass plantings for the best show.

planting.note: Start indoors early; surface-sow as seeds need light to germinate.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, as light is needed for germination, then transplant after frost. This tall, statuesque plant bears large leaves and drooping clusters of long, tubular white flowers that turn very fragrant in the evening. It shines in a white or moon garden alongside delphiniums or other tall companions. Give it rich, moist soil and part shade in hot climates, and enjoy the perfume from late afternoon into night.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall; blooms first year from an early start.

directions: Sow seeds barely covered in spring or late summer; this variety is known to bloom in its first season from an early start. Tall spires of single and semi-double pink, rose, and white flowers evoke a classic 1800s English cottage garden. Provide deep, rich soil and ample moisture for best performance, though established plants tolerate some drought. Stake tall stems in windy sites and allow plants to self-sow to maintain the planting year to year.

planting.note: Start indoors, surface-sowing since light aids germination, or fall sow.

directions: Surface-sow the seed since light aids germination, starting indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost or fall sowing outdoors. Large single and semi-double golden flowers with dark centers stand tall in heat and drought without staking. They are excellent, long-lasting cut flowers and lure butterflies while providing seeds for birds in winter. Grow in full sun and average, well-drained soil, and deadhead to prolong the extended bloom season.

planting.note: Plant the potted rose in spring after hard frosts; sold as a live plant.

directions: Plant this floribunda rose in spring in a hole amended with compost, keeping the graft at soil level in colder zones. Clusters of richly fragrant purple-plum blooms appear from spring through fall on an elegant upright form, making a bold focal point. Provide full sun, well-drained soil, and consistent moisture, watering at the base to keep foliage dry. Prune in late winter to shape the plant and deadhead spent clusters to encourage repeat flowering.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, then start indoors; benefits from a chilling period.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds in the refrigerator for several weeks, then sow indoors or direct sow in fall for natural chilling. This swamp milkweed blend produces clusters of pink and rose blooms that are irresistible to monarch butterflies and hummingbirds. It grows best in consistently moist to wet soils such as near a pond or rain garden but adapts to average beds. As a host plant for monarch caterpillars, leave foliage intact and avoid pesticides to support the life cycle.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow in spring.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow in spring, barely covering it. Long, wiry stems carry pincushion blooms in white, lilac, and blue shades that are outstanding and long-lasting in bouquets. This tough, fairly drought-tolerant, hardy plant has few pests and blooms over an extended season. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and cut or deadhead frequently to keep flowers coming.

common_name: Javelin Forte Deep Rose Spanish Lavender

planting.note: Plant the live starter after frost danger passes; sold as a live plant.

directions: Plant this compact Spanish lavender in spring in lean, sharply drained soil after frost danger has passed. Deep rose-pink bracts crown purple flowers above classic silver-green foliage on a tidy, mounded plant ideal for containers and borders. Give it full sun, avoid heavy or wet soils, and water sparingly once established since it is drought-tolerant. Shear lightly after the main flush to keep the plant compact and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost; dislikes root disturbance.

directions: Direct sow the large seeds 1/2 to 1 inch deep after the last frost, as nasturtiums resent transplanting. This compact jewel-toned blend makes an excellent edging plant and produces edible flowers and leaves with a peppery flavor for salads. Plants are fairly drought-tolerant and actually bloom best in lean soil, since rich soil favors foliage over flowers. Provide full sun to part shade, and grow in a sunny window for winter color indoors.

planting.note: Start indoors in darkness; violas prefer cool spring and fall weather.

directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, or direct sow in cool spring or fall weather. These charming purple, yellow, and white faces bloom prolifically and can poke up above light snow. Though perennial in zones 4-8, they are often grown as annuals since they falter in extended heat. Grow in part shade with moist soil, use them as spring bulb cover, and let them self-sow to return year after year.

planting.note: Start indoors in darkness; seeds need darkness to germinate.

directions: Sow the fine seed in darkness indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Also called painted tongue, this variety bears sprays of velvety, deep-blue, trumpet-shaped flowers from summer until frost. It adds rich depth to mixed containers, borders, and cut arrangements while attracting pollinators. Provide full sun, rich well-drained soil, and support for the somewhat lax stems, deadheading to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil, covering seeds about 1/4 inch, or start indoors a few weeks early. These 4 to 5 inch lime-green and frothy-white blooms dazzle in cottage-garden borders or massed with bachelor's buttons and snapdragons. The edible petals can dress up salads and desserts, and the long stems make superb cut flowers. Grow in full sun with good air circulation to reduce mildew, and cut or deadhead often to keep blooms coming.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow when warm.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow when soil is warm, covering about 1/4 inch. This historic variety, the first white marigold introduced in 1975, produces large creamy-white blooms that are rare in the marigold world. Sturdy plants thrive in full sun and average soil and reward regular deadheading with continuous flowers. Grow near vegetables where they may help deter pests, and cut blooms for long-lasting bouquets.

planting.note: Start indoors in darkness; violas thrive in cool spring and fall.

directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, or direct sow in cool spring or fall weather. 'King Henry' forms neat little mounds of richly colored royal-purple flowers ideal for garden edges and containers. Violas love cool weather, making them perfect for spring bulb cover or fall color after annuals fade. Grow in part shade with moist soil, deadhead to prolong bloom, and expect self-sowing in favorable spots.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow in spring.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow in spring, barely covering it. Opulent apricot-toned double blooms stand tall and firm on strong stems, adding elegance to beds and bouquets. China asters make excellent, long-lasting cut flowers and appreciate full sun and rich, well-drained soil. Rotate planting sites each year to avoid soilborne disease, and stagger sowings for a longer season of blooms.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, pressing it in since light aids germination. Papery silvery-rose blooms open into delicate crowns that hold their color beautifully when dried. Well-branched plants reach a good height and supply abundant stems for fresh and everlasting arrangements. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and cut flowers just before they fully open for the best dried results.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, pressing it in since light aids germination. Stately blooms unfurl in layers of soft silvery-white petals for a regal presence in beds and bouquets. Tall, well-branched plants are drought-tolerant once established and provide abundant stems for cutting and drying. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and harvest flowers just as they begin to open for the best dried displays.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This ornamental oregano drapes in cascading layers of papery green and rosy-purple hop-like bracts that catch the light. Drought-tolerant and pollinator-friendly, it excels in containers, bouquets, and the front of sunny borders. Grow in full sun with sharply drained soil, avoid overwatering, and cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements.

planting.note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; likes cool weather.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. This shorter blend delivers a rainbow of lightly scented blooms on knee-high vines that need little or no support. It is ideal for a short trellis, hanging basket, or the front of a border and delights pollinators. Keep roots cool and evenly watered, and cut flowers regularly to prolong the display before summer heat.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This monarda is covered with tiered whorls of lavender-pink flower clusters that attract butterflies, hummingbirds, and bees. The edible petals can replace oregano in savory dishes, be steeped into tea, or float in punch. Grow in full sun to part shade with good air circulation to limit mildew, and deadhead to extend flowering.

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface in early spring or late fall; needs light and cold.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed in early spring or late fall directly where plants are to grow, since poppies dislike transplanting and need light to germinate. 'Lauren's Grape' produces rich purple single blooms followed by attractive seed pods prized for arrangements and baking. Thin seedlings to give each plant room, and grow in full sun with well-drained soil. Allow some pods to mature and self-sow to bring the poppies back each year.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; loves heat.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant once the weather is warm. 'Lavender Lady' produces abundant papery, clover-like lavender blooms that shrug off extreme heat and drought once established. The long-lasting flowers are excellent for fresh bouquets and dry beautifully for everlasting arrangements. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and pinch young plants to encourage branching and more blooms.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow when warm.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow when soil is warm, covering lightly. These bushy, compact signet marigolds form dense mounds of lacy, scented foliage topped with profuse single lemon and tangerine blooms. The petite flowers are edible and can be infused into oil or vinegar for salad dressings. Grow in full sun with average soil, deadhead to keep blooms coming, and use them as tidy edging or rock-garden accents.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow when warm.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost or direct sow when soil warms, covering about 1/4 inch. 'Lemon Drop' bears cheerful sunny-yellow flowers on compact plants perfect for the front of the bed, patio pots, and window boxes. It takes little space in the vegetable garden and may help reduce soil nematodes. Grow in full sun with average soil, and deadhead regularly to encourage continuous bloom through the season.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost; succession sow for continuous bloom.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after the last frost, and sow again every 2 to 4 weeks until three months before first fall frost for continuous bloom. Majestic multi-branched plants produce many soft lemon-yellow flowers ideal for cutting all summer. Easy to grow in almost any well-drained soil, these sunflowers are drought-tolerant once established. Give them full sun, and leave late seed heads for birds to enjoy in autumn.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil, covering seeds about 1/4 inch, or start indoors a few weeks early. 'Lilliput' produces a never-ending show of vibrant, small double pompon flowers on long stems all summer. Cutting the blooms for bouquets encourages the plants to produce even more, delighting butterflies and bees. Grow in full sun with good air circulation to limit mildew, and deadhead or harvest often to keep it flowering.

planting.note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; prefers cool weather.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. Unlike vining sweet peas, 'Little Sweetheart' is a dwarf bush type with profuse, lightly scented small flowers in a rainbow of colors. Its compact habit suits hanging baskets, patio containers, window boxes, and the front of a border. Keep roots cool and evenly moist, and cut blooms often to prolong flowering before summer heat.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring; nigella dislikes transplanting.

directions: Direct sow in early spring where plants are to grow, since nigella resents transplanting. 'Delft Blue' bears distinctive dual-colored blooms of violet-blue over snowy white with burgundy stamens amid feathery foliage. Drought-tolerant and easy to grow, it is perfect for borders and attracts pollinators, while the ornamental seed pods are prized for arrangements. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, and let some pods mature so plants self-sow for next year.

planting.note: Sow in darkness indoors; seeds need darkness to germinate.

directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Above slim, silvery foliage, tiny star-shaped blooms form 2 to 3 inch pincushion-like flowerheads with a light scent that draws pollinators. This Australian wildflower is a soft, fragrant beauty that makes a lovely, airy cut flower. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil, provide support in windy sites, and harvest stems for delicate bouquets.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; snapdragons like cool weather.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This dwarf snapdragon blend offers a lovely mix of colors that charms in flower beds, borders, containers, and hanging baskets. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so transplant hardened seedlings out around the last frost. Grow in full sun to part shade with well-drained soil, pinch young plants to encourage branching, and deadhead to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors since light aids germination; prefers cool, moist conditions.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, since light aids germination. This mix dazzles with blooms in yellow, orange, pink, and red on plants ideal for borders, containers, and wildflower gardens. Monkey flower thrives in cool, moist conditions and blooms continuously from spring into summer. Grow in sun to part shade, keep the soil consistently moist, and deadhead to keep the colorful show going.

planting.note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; likes cool weather.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. Mammoth sweet peas boast extra-large, sweetly scented flowers on long stems that are perfect for cutting. Bred from an Early Multiflora Gigantea Spencer type, this blend offers extra drought and heat resistance for a longer season. Provide a tall trellis, keep roots cool and evenly moist, and cut flowers frequently to prolong the bloom.

planting.note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; likes cool weather.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep in cool, moist soil in early spring. This Mammoth selection produces striking, extra-large blooms in deep velvety purple-blue atop long stems ideal for cutting gardens. Bred from Early Multiflora Gigantea, it offers Spencer-type beauty with added drought and heat resistance. Provide a tall trellis, keep roots cool and evenly watered, and cut flowers often to extend flowering.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after the last frost, spacing plants well to support their large size. This giant grows fast on a single stalk that can reach 12 feet tall and produces one enormous 10 to 12 inch flower head. Not fussy about soil and drought-tolerant, it makes an attractive screen when grown in a tight row. Sow monthly for continual bloom, and leave the seed heads for birds and for harvesting edible seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost; soak seed to speed germination.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow after the last frost, covering about 1/2 inch. Named for opening in late afternoon, this fast-growing plant flourishes almost anywhere, including hot, windy spots, and tolerates most soils. Fragrant tubular flowers open after 4pm to attract hummingbirds and other pollinators, making it an excellent annual hedge. It reseeds freely and forms tubers that can be dug and stored over winter in cold climates.

planting.note: Sow in darkness indoors; pansies thrive in cool spring and fall weather.

directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Matrix Red Blotch bears extra-large 3.5 inch scarlet-red flowers with dramatic dark blotches on compact, well-branched plants. Pansies love cool weather, so plant them out in early spring or fall for lush, tidy color. Grow in sun to part shade with moist, rich soil, and deadhead the edible blooms to keep flowering strong.

planting.note: Sow in darkness indoors; pansies thrive in cool spring and fall weather.

directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Matrix Rose Blotch features soft rose petals with dark centers plus occasional creamy-rose blooms for elegant color. Its compact, spreading habit gives lush coverage with little upkeep, and the edible flowers brighten dishes and drinks. Grow in sun to part shade with moist, rich soil, planting in early spring or fall and deadheading to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Sow in darkness indoors; pansies thrive in cool spring and fall weather.

directions: Sow seed in darkness indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost, since light inhibits germination. Matrix Solar Flare dazzles with red, orange, and yellow blooms and burgundy centers outlined in golden yellow. The color shifts with temperature, showing more red in cool weather and brighter yellow tones as it warms. Grow in sun to part shade with moist, rich soil, planting in early spring or fall and deadheading to keep the display vivid.
